content.assessment: This proposal has strong institutional grounding from this lens because it formalizes a mechanism the ministry has already exercised: discretionary approval power used to deny expansion on segregation grounds, as documented in the Tiszavasvári case (F-live-hungarian_education_system-09), and it is supported by a clear documented causal channel in the Encs case showing that track expansion approval directly produces creaming of higher-status pupils from feeder schools (F-live-hungarian_education_system-07). This gives the proposal an unusually strong administrative-compatibility basis, since it extends existing precedent rather than inventing new authority. From a system-coverage standpoint, however, the proposal is explicitly preventive only, meaning it does not touch already-existing 6/8-year tracks, the underlying exam mechanism (F-live-hungarian_education_system-02), or free school choice (F-live-hungarian_education_system-03), all of which remain fully unchanged and continue generating the documented composition gaps (F-live-hungarian_education_system-04) and EU-topping segregation levels (F-live-hungarian_education_system-06).
opportunities: Aligns with the ministry's demonstrated willingness to use discretionary refusal on segregation grounds, suggesting institutional appetite already exists for at least the marginal/preventive version of this tool (F-live-hungarian_education_system-09)
Could be paired with the ministry's broader review of the entrance-exam system as a complementary administrative track (F-live-hungarian_education_system-10)
strengths: Directly extends an existing, already-used ministerial precedent (Tiszavasvári refusal) rather than requiring new legal authority (F-live-hungarian_education_system-09)

Supported by a concrete documented causal case (Encs) showing expansion approval produces measurable creaming in feeder schools, giving the mechanism an evidentiary basis specific to Hungarian institutional practice (F-live-hungarian_education_system-07)
threats: Discretion has so far been exercised inconsistently across cases (approved historically in some contexts per the three-track coexistence since the 1990s, denied in Tiszavasvári), raising a legitimacy risk if a formalized test is applied unevenly (F-live-hungarian_education_system-01, F-live-hungarian_education_system-09)
weaknesses: Purely preventive scope leaves the entire existing stock of 6/8-year tracks, the exam mechanism, and free choice untouched, so current segregation levels (F-live-hungarian_education_system-06) and composition gaps (F-live-hungarian_education_system-04) are not addressed for already-approved provision
